info.text: CSIR NET level MCQ questions on Inner Product Spaces

questiontext.text: In an inner product space, the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality states \(|\langle u,v\rangle| \leq \|u\|\|v\|\). Equality holds iff:

answer.text: \(u\) and \(v\) are linearly dependent (one is a scalar multiple of the other)

feedback.text: Correct. Equality in Cauchy-Schwarz holds iff \(u = \alpha v\) for some scalar \(\alpha\), i.e., \(u\) and \(v\) are linearly dependent.
Incorrect. Equal norms do not imply equality in Cauchy-Schwarz. Equality requires linear dependence.
Incorrect. If \(u\perp v\), then \(|\langle u,v\rangle|=0\leq\|u\|\|v\|\), which is equality only if at least one vector is zero.
Incorrect. \(\langle u,v\rangle=0\) means orthogonality, which gives \(0\leq\|u\|\|v\|\), equality only when one is zero.

questiontext.text: Applying Gram-Schmidt to \(\{1, x, x^2\}\) in \(L^2[0,1]\) with inner product \(\langle f,g\rangle=\int_0^1f(x)g(x)\,dx\), the first two orthonormal polynomials are \(e_1=1\) and \(e_2=\sqrt{3}(2x-1)\). The third orthonormal polynomial \(e_3\) is a scalar multiple of:

feedback.text: Correct. After subtracting components along \(e_1\) and \(e_2\) from \(x^2\), one obtains (up to normalization) the shifted Legendre polynomial \(6x^2-6x+1\).
Incorrect. This is not orthogonal to \(e_1=1\): \(\int_0^1(x^2-x)\,dx = 1/3-1/2 = -1/6\neq0\).
Incorrect. \(\int_0^1(x^2+x-1)\,dx = 1/3+1/2-1=-1/6\neq0\). Not orthogonal to \(e_1\).

questiontext.text: Let \(W\) be a subspace of an inner product space \(V\). Which of the following is true about \(W^\perp\)?

answer.text: If \(V\) is finite-dimensional, then \(V = W \oplus W^\perp\) and \(\dim W + \dim W^\perp = \dim V\)

feedback.text: Correct. Every finite-dimensional inner product space decomposes as the direct sum of any subspace and its orthogonal complement.
Incorrect. \(W\cap W^\perp = \{0\}\): a nonzero vector cannot be orthogonal to itself (in a real inner product space with positive definiteness).
Incorrect. In finite dimensions, \((W^\perp)^\perp = W\) (the complement of the complement returns to \(W\)).
Incorrect. \(\dim W^\perp = \dim V - \dim W\).

questiontext.text: The parallelogram law states: in any inner product space, \(\|u+v\|^2 + \|u-v\|^2 = 2(\|u\|^2 + \|v\|^2)\). This law characterizes inner product spaces among normed spaces. If a normed space satisfies the parallelogram law, then the inner product is recovered by:

feedback.text: Correct. The polarization identity recovers the inner product from the norm in a real inner product space.
Incorrect. This is the Cauchy-Schwarz bound, not the definition of the inner product.
Incorrect. This equals \(2\langle u,v\rangle\) by the expansion \(\|u+v\|^2=\|u\|^2+2\langle u,v\rangle+\|v\|^2\).
Incorrect. This is just the average of the squared norms, independent of the angle between \(u\) and \(v\).

questiontext.text: Let \(W\) be a closed subspace of a Hilbert space \(H\). For any \(v\in H\), the best approximation to \(v\) in \(W\) is the unique \(w^*\in W\) that minimizes \(\|v-w\|\). This \(w^*\) is characterized by:

answer.text: \(v - w^* \in W^\perp\) (the residual is orthogonal to \(W\))

feedback.text: Correct. The orthogonal projection theorem: \(w^*=\text{proj}_W v\) is the unique element satisfying \(\langle v-w^*, w\rangle=0\) for all \(w\in W\).
Incorrect. The residual must be in \(W^\perp\), not in \(W\) itself.
Incorrect. By Pythagoras: \(\|v\|^2 = \|w^*\|^2 + \|v-w^*\|^2\), so \(\|w^*\|\leq\|v\|\).
Incorrect — wait, actually this IS true (if \(v\in W\), then the projection is \(v\) itself). But this answer is incomplete. The characterization is \(v-w^*\perp W\).

questiontext.text: The Riesz representation theorem for Hilbert spaces states: for every bounded linear functional \(f: H\to\mathbb{F}\) on a Hilbert space \(H\), there exists a unique \(y\in H\) such that:

answer.text: \(f(x) = \langle x, y\rangle\) for all \(x\in H\), with \(\|f\| = \|y\|\)

feedback.text: Correct. Every bounded linear functional on a Hilbert space is represented by an inner product with a unique element, and the norm of the functional equals the norm of the representing element.
Incorrect. Linear functionals map to scalars, not vectors. The representation is \(f(x)=\langle x,y\rangle\).
Incorrect. The Riesz theorem identifies \(f\) with the inner product \(\langle\cdot,y\rangle\), not a transpose.
Incorrect. This is the Cauchy-Schwarz upper bound, not the representation formula.

questiontext.text: Let \(\{e_k\}_{k=1}^\infty\) be an orthonormal sequence in a Hilbert space \(H\). Bessel's inequality states: for any \(x\in H\),

feedback.text: Correct. Bessel's inequality: the sum of squares of Fourier coefficients is bounded by the squared norm. Equality (Parseval's identity) holds iff \(\{e_k\}\) is a complete orthonormal system.
Incorrect. This is Parseval's identity, which holds only when \(\{e_k\}\) is an orthonormal basis (complete system). Bessel's inequality gives \(\leq\).
Incorrect. Bessel's inequality involves the sum of squares \(|\langle x,e_k\rangle|^2\), not the sum of absolute values.
Incorrect. The inequality is \(\leq\), not \(\geq\).

questiontext.text: The standard orthonormal basis for \(L^2[-\pi,\pi]\) (with inner product \(\langle f,g\rangle=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f\bar{g}\,dx\)) consists of:

feedback.text: Correct. The complex exponentials \(\{e^{inx}/\sqrt{2\pi}\}_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}\) (or equivalently with the normalized inner product, just \(\{e^{inx}\}\)) form a complete orthonormal basis for \(L^2[-\pi,\pi]\).
Incorrect. The monomials \(\{x^n\}\) are not orthogonal on \([-\pi,\pi]\) and form a basis only after Gram-Schmidt (yielding Legendre polynomials).
Incorrect. Only sines cannot span \(L^2[-\pi,\pi]\); constant functions and cosines are not in the span of pure sines.
Incorrect. Gaussians do not form an orthonormal basis for \(L^2[-\pi,\pi]\).

questiontext.text: For bounded linear operators \(S, T\) on a Hilbert space \(H\), which of the following is NOT always true?

feedback.text: Correct (this is FALSE in general). The correct formula is \((ST)^* = T^*S^*\) (order reverses), not \(S^*T^*\).
Incorrect (this IS true). The adjoint of the adjoint is the original operator.
Incorrect (this IS true). The adjoint of a sum is the sum of adjoints.
Incorrect (this IS true). Scalar multiplication conjugates when taking adjoints.

questiontext.text: The orthogonal projection of \(v\) onto a nonzero vector \(u\) in an inner product space is:

feedback.text: Correct. The projection formula: the component of \(v\) along \(u\) is \(\frac{\langle v,u\rangle}{\|u\|^2}u\).
Incorrect. This divides by \(\|v\|^2\) instead of \(\|u\|^2\) and projects in the wrong direction.
Incorrect. This is missing the normalization \(1/\|u\|^2\). It equals the projection only when \(u\) is a unit vector.

answer.text: \(\text{proj}_u v = \|v\|\cos\theta \cdot u\) where \(\theta\) is the angle

feedback.text: Incorrect (almost, but missing normalization of \(u\)). The correct formula is \(\text{proj}_u v = \frac{\langle v,u\rangle}{\|u\|^2}u\).

name.text: LA12-Q11 Spectral Theorem for Normal Operators

questiontext.text: The spectral theorem for normal operators on a finite-dimensional complex inner product space states:

answer.text: Every normal operator is unitarily diagonalizable (there exists an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors)

feedback.text: Correct. Over \(\mathbb{C}\), a matrix is unitarily diagonalizable iff it is normal (\(TT^*=T^*T\)). This includes Hermitian, unitary, and skew-Hermitian matrices.

answer.text: Every normal operator has all real eigenvalues

feedback.text: Incorrect. Normal operators can have complex eigenvalues (e.g., unitary operators have eigenvalues on the unit circle).

answer.text: Every normal operator is the identity

feedback.text: Incorrect. Unitary, Hermitian, and many other operators are normal but not the identity.

answer.text: Normal operators have no eigenvalues in infinite dimensions

feedback.text: Incorrect. The spectral theorem applies to normal operators in infinite dimensions too (via the spectral measure), and normal operators can have eigenvalues.

questiontext.text: The Gram matrix of vectors \(v_1,\ldots,v_k\) in an inner product space is \(G_{ij}=\langle v_i,v_j\rangle\). The vectors \(v_1,\ldots,v_k\) are linearly independent iff:

feedback.text: Correct. The vectors are linearly independent iff the Gram matrix \(G\) is positive definite (equivalently, \(\det G > 0\)).
Incorrect. \(\det G = 0\) indicates linear dependence, not independence.
Incorrect. \(G=I_k\) means the vectors are orthonormal, which is stronger than linear independence.
Incorrect. Positive trace only requires at least one nonzero vector; it doesn't guarantee linear independence.

questiontext.text: A linear map \(T: V\to W\) between inner product spaces is an isometry if \(\|Tv\| = \|v\|\) for all \(v\in V\). Which is equivalent to \(T\) being an isometry?

feedback.text: Correct. Preserving the inner product (and hence all angles and lengths) is equivalent to being an isometry, by the polarization identity.
Incorrect. An isometry need not be surjective (e.g., the right shift on \(\ell^2\)).
Incorrect. \(T^*T=I\) (i.e., \(T\) is an isometry) but \(T^*=T^{-1}\) requires surjectivity (i.e., \(T\) is unitary), which is stronger.
Incorrect. The operator norm of an isometry is 1 (since \(\|Tv\|=\|v\|\) implies \(\|T\|=1\)).

questiontext.text: Every matrix \(A\) with linearly independent columns has a QR decomposition \(A = QR\) where \(Q\) has orthonormal columns and \(R\) is upper triangular with positive diagonal. This decomposition arises from:

answer.text: Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization applied to the columns of \(A\)

feedback.text: Correct. The Gram-Schmidt process applied to the columns of \(A\) produces the orthonormal columns of \(Q\); the relations between original and orthonormal columns give \(R\).
Incorrect. The eigenvalue decomposition of \(A^TA\) gives the SVD-related matrices, not directly the QR decomposition.
Incorrect. Row reduction gives the LU decomposition, not QR.
Incorrect. The spectral decomposition \(A=PDP^{-1}\) applies to diagonalizable matrices and is different from QR.

questiontext.text: In \(\mathbb{R}^n\), the angle \(\theta\) between nonzero vectors \(u\) and \(v\) satisfies \(\cos\theta = \frac{\langle u,v\rangle}{\|u\|\|v\|}\). The angle between \(u=(1,1,0)\) and \(v=(1,0,1)\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. \(\langle u,v\rangle=1\cdot1+1\cdot0+0\cdot1=1\). \(\|u\|=\sqrt{2}\), \(\|v\|=\sqrt{2}\). So \(\cos\theta=1/2\), giving \(\theta=\pi/3\).
Incorrect. \(\cos\theta=1/2\neq0\), so the vectors are not orthogonal.
Incorrect. \(\cos(\pi/4)=1/\sqrt{2}\neq1/2\).
Incorrect. \(\cos(\pi/6)=\sqrt{3}/2\neq1/2\).

questiontext.text: The Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse \(A^+\) of a matrix \(A\) with SVD \(A = U\Sigma V^T\) is defined as:

answer.text: \(A^+ = V\Sigma^+ U^T\) where \(\Sigma^+\) replaces each nonzero singular value \(\sigma_i\) by \(1/\sigma_i\)

feedback.text: Correct. The pseudoinverse inverts the nonzero singular values and transposes \(U\) and \(V\): \(A^+ = V\Sigma^+U^T\).
Incorrect. \((A^TA)^{-1}A^T\) is the left pseudoinverse and equals \(A^+\) only when \(A\) has full column rank.
Incorrect. The correct formula has \(V\) and \(U^T\), not \(U\) and \(V^T\): \(A^+=V\Sigma^+U^T\).
Incorrect. The pseudoinverse equals the transpose only for orthogonal matrices.

name.text: LA12-Q17 Tensor Product of Inner Product Spaces

questiontext.text: If \(V\) and \(W\) are inner product spaces with dimensions \(m\) and \(n\), the tensor product \(V\otimes W\) has:

answer.text: Dimension \(mn\) with inner product \(\langle v_1\otimes w_1, v_2\otimes w_2\rangle = \langle v_1,v_2\rangle_V\langle w_1,w_2\rangle_W\)

feedback.text: Correct. The tensor product space has dimension \(mn\) and inherits a natural inner product from the factor spaces.

answer.text: Dimension \(m+n\) with inner product from direct sum

feedback.text: Incorrect. The direct sum \(V\oplus W\) has dimension \(m+n\); the tensor product \(V\otimes W\) has dimension \(mn\).
Incorrect. The tensor product of vector spaces of dimensions \(m\) and \(n\) has dimension \(mn\), not \(m^n\).
Incorrect. The inner product on \(V\otimes W\) is defined by the bilinear extension of the product of inner products on \(V\) and \(W\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following inner product spaces is NOT a Hilbert space (i.e., is not complete)?

answer.text: The space of polynomials \(P[0,1]\) with \(\langle f,g\rangle = \int_0^1 fg\,dx\)

feedback.text: Correct. The polynomial space is not complete: a sequence of polynomials can converge in \(L^2\) to a non-polynomial function (e.g., \(e^x\) is the \(L^2\) limit of its Taylor partial sums).
Incorrect. \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is complete (every Cauchy sequence of real \(n\)-tuples converges), hence a Hilbert space.
Incorrect. \(L^2[0,1]\) is complete (the Riesz-Fischer theorem), hence a Hilbert space.
Incorrect. \(\ell^2\) is complete (a Cauchy sequence in \(\ell^2\) converges coordinatewise to an \(\ell^2\) element).

questiontext.text: If \(\{e_k\}\) is a complete orthonormal basis of a Hilbert space \(H\) and \(x\in H\), the Fourier coefficients \(c_k = \langle x,e_k\rangle\) satisfy:

feedback.text: Correct. By Parseval's identity \(\sum|c_k|^2=\|x\|^2<\infty\), so the terms of a convergent series must tend to zero: \(c_k\to0\).
Incorrect. Parseval gives \(\sum|c_k|^2<\infty\) (square summability), but not \(\sum|c_k|<\infty\) in general.

answer.text: All \(c_k\) are zero except finitely many

feedback.text: Incorrect. This would mean \(x\) is in the linear span of finitely many \(e_k\). In general, \(x\) can have infinitely many nonzero Fourier coefficients.
Incorrect. The Fourier coefficients tend to 0, not to \(\|x\|\).

questiontext.text: Let \(W = \text{span}\{(1,1,1)\}\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\). The orthogonal projection of \(v=(1,2,3)\) onto \(W\) is:

feedback.text: Incorrect. The projection onto \(W\) is not \(v\) itself (unless \(v\in W\), which requires \(v=(c,c,c)\) for some \(c\)).
Incorrect. This is \(v - (1,1,1) = v - u\), not the projection.
